rulings.Case Title: FREDERICK C FIECHTER VS. CARL WESCOTT et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Frederick Fiechter Motion For Assignment And Charging Orders
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, November 18, 2019, Line 8, Plaintiff Frederick Fiechter Motion For Assignment And Charging Orders Plaintiff Frederick C. Fiechter's motion for assignment and charging orders against Defendant Carl Wescott is granted. Plaintiff holds two judgments against Defendant, a vexatious litigant: (1) a July 14, 2015 judgment in the amount of $569,901.33 in principal, accrued interest as of July 24, 2015 in the amount of $407,050.19, and $513,080.79 in attorney's fees and costs; and (2) a September 6, 2011 judgment, renewed on August 28, 2015 in the total amount of $1,822,845.40. Although Defendant apparently served opposition papers on Plaintiff, no opposition was filed with the Court, nor did Plaintiff deliver courtesy copies of any opposition to the Court, as required by the Local Rules. Accordingly, the motion is deemed unopposed. A hearing is required regarding the scope of the requested orders, specifically regarding whether the Court may properly issue orders directed to third parties not before the Court who were not given notice of the motion. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: MILES HENRY COBB SELLAI VS. CALIFORNIA PACIFIC MEDICAL CENTER ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Sutter Bay Hospitals Notice Of Motion And Motion Contesting Determination Of Good Faith Settlement By Non-Party San Francisco Ear, Nose Throat Medical Group, Inc. As Requested In Defendant Dr. Hartmans Application For Good Faith Settlement Determination
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, November 18, 2019, Line 2, DEFENDANT SUTTER BAY HOSPITALS DBA CALIFORNIA PACIFIC'S Motion Contesting Determination Of Good Faith Settlement By Non-Party San Francisco Ear, Nose Throat Medical Group, Inc. As Requested In Defendant Dr. Hartmans Application For Good Faith Settlement Determination Defendant Sutter Bay Hospitals' motion contesting determination of good faith settlement as to San Francisco Ear, Nose, & Throat Medical Group ("SF ENT") is denied. Sutter Bay is correct that by statute, a good faith settlement determination is only available to a "party to an action" or "a settling party." (Code Civ. Proc. § 877.6(a)(1),(2).) It is undisputed that SF ENT was never formally made a party to this action. (See Cal. Rules of Court, rule 1.6(15) [a party "is a person appearing in an action."].) However, Dr. Hartman and SF ENT show that in May 2018, plaintiff served on all parties an amendment to the complaint naming SF ENT as a Doe defendant, although that amendment mistakenly was not filed with the Court; that the settlement discussions with Plaintiff concerned both Dr. Hartman and SF ENT; that they are jointly insured pursuant to a single insurance policy that provides coverage to SF ENT to the extent that its alleged liability is claimed to arise from Dr. Hartman's acts or omissions; that Plaintiff agreed in settling the case to a full release of both Dr. Hartman and also SF ENT, as well as a dismissal of Dr. Engel, the other SF ENT physician named in the suit; and that the $1 million policy limits settlement exhausts all of the coverage available jointly to Dr. Hartman and SF ENT under that policy. (Mintz Decl. 6, 7, 12, 13; Sparer Decl. 3-5 & Ex. A.) Under the circumstances, the Court deems the complaint amended nunc pro tunc to name SF ENT as a defendant, and finds that the settlement reached between Plaintiff, by and through his guardian ad litem, and Dr. Hartman and SF ENT was entered into in good faith and is a good faith settlement within the meaning of Code Civ. Proc. §§ 877 and 877.6. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The Court's October 30, 2019 order granting the application of Dr. Hartman and SF ENT for a good faith determination, which was entered in error, is vacated. Counsel for Dr. Hartman and SF ENT is required to prepare a new proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, November 18, 2019, Line 3, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO's Motion For Sanctions [Discovery] Pro Tem Judge Robert Kane, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: CCSF's Motion to Compel Compliance with Court Order, etc. continued to December 2, 2019 at 9:00 a.m. in Dept. 301 before Pro Tem Judge Scott Borrowman. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to rkane1089@aol.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: MOHAMMAD JOIYAH VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motions And Motions To Compel Plaintiffs Responses To Form Interrogatories, Employment Law, Set One And To Compel Plaintiffs Further Responses To Request For Production Of Documents, Set One
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, November 18, 2019, Line 4, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO's Motions To Compel Plaintiffs Responses To Form Interrogatories, Employment Law, Set One And To Compel Plaintiffs Further Responses To Request For Production Of Documents, Set One. Pro Tem Judge Robert Kane, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: CCSF's Motion to Compel. Parties to appear. Court intends to granted motion to compel answers to form interrogatories, without objection, to be served within 15 days of entry of this order and parties to appear to discuss appropriate amount of sanctions and to whom they should be assessed. With respect to the requests for production, RFP 1-16 appear to center around the meaning of "such" and parties are to be prepared to discuss why that is not a code compliant response in light of the objections and whether all documents not subject to an alleged privilege have been produced. With respect to the objections, they appear to center around whether documents are privileged under the Unemployment Insurance Code or Evidence Code §1040 in so far as the documents were generated by a public agency. The case of Crest Catering Co. v. Sup. Ct. (1965) 62 Cal.2d 274 is the basis for plaintiff's claim of privilege. Crest Catering dealt with tax returns submitted by an employer, not the employee. (See Wilson v. Sup. Ct. (1976) 103 Cal.App.3d. 825, 828-830.) This interpretation was adopted by the federal district court in Teutscher cited by CCSF. With respect to section 1040, it is unclear whether plaintiff has documents generated by a public entity that would otherwise be privileged that he is withholding. With respect to RFP 18, it appears that plaintiff has produced all documents; it appears the objections may not apply. RFP 21 and 23 see comments regarding RFP 1-16 above. Parties should be prepared to address these issues. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to rkane1089@aol.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: MOHAMMAD JOIYAH VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order Compelling Further Responses To Interrogatories And Request For Production Of Documents And For Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, November 18, 2019, Line 4, DEFENDANTs FINE ARTS MUSEUMS OF SAN FRANCISCO and CORPORATION OF FINE ARTS MUSEUMS' Motion For Order Compelling Further Responses To Interrogatories And Request For Production Of Documents And For Request For Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Robert Kane, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Corp. of Fine Art's Motion to Compel. Parties to appear. With respect to the Form Interrogatories, discovery is broadly defined. (CCP §2017.010.) Plaintiff's complaint arises out of an employment relationship. As a result, it appears that interrogatories 200.3, 200.4, 200.5, appear to be relevant under this broad definition even though the claims are based on FEHA. Interrogatory 201.3 does not appear to be directed at either employee or employer but isn't this duplicative of interrogatory 202.1. It is not clear whether this interrogatory was answered as the complete set of responses was not attached to the Kim declaration. Interrogatory 201.4 appears to directed solely to the employer as it require information that would not be in the possession of employee. With respect to interrogatory 210.2, plaintiff's response was misstated in the Separate Statement. Moreover, there is reference that plaintiff is not seeking any economic damages, if so, is this relevant? With respect to interrogatories 213.1 and 213.2, if plaintiff is only seeking emotional distress damages is this relevant. (Tentative ruling continued, see next entry.)

rulings.Case Title: MOHAMMAD JOIYAH VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants Fine Arts Museums Of San Francisco And Corporation Of Fine Arts Museums' Motion For Order Compelling Further Responses To Interrogatories And Request For Production Of Documents And For Request For Sanctions (Part 2 For Tentative Ruling Only)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, November 18, 2019, Line 4, Defendants Fine Arts Museums Of San Francisco And Corporation Of Fine Arts Museums' Motion For Order Compelling Further Responses To Interrogatories And Request For Production Of Documents And For Request For Sanctions (Part 2 For Tentative Ruling Only) (Tentative ruling continued from previous entry.) Finally, with respect to special interrogatories 1-3 regarding plaintiff's attorney fees and costs, the court could not find any cases directly on point regarding pretrial discovery of the amounts of attorney fees, hours and costs. This information would certainly be relevant to the subject matter as it is information that would facilitate settlement. (See Garamendi v. Golden Eagle Ins. Co. (2004) 116 Cal.App.4th 694, 712 n. 8.) It is unclear whether disclosure at this stage of the proceedings would be protected as work protect if there is no disclosure as to what was done only for the amounts. Parties should also be prepared to take about whether sanctions should be imposed and, if so, in what amount Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to rkane1089@aol.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: DONALD R MCCAFFREE ET AL VS. VOLKSWAGEN GROUP OF AMERICA INC. A NEW JERSEY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Request For Production From Defendant Volkswagen Group Of America, Inc., And Request For Sanctions; Memorandum In Support Of Motion
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, November 18, 2019, Line 2, PLAINTIFFs DONALD MCCAFFREE and SARA MCCAFFREE's Motion To Compel Further Responses To Request For Production From Defendant Volkswagen Group Of America, Inc., And Request For Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Robert Kane, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Parties to appear. Plaintiff's Motion to Compel seeks to have defendant produce documents regarding other claims related to alleged problems with engines similar to those in plaintiff's vehicle. The complaint alleges that plaintiff's vehicle is defective because of these engine problems which apparently have yet to manifest themselves. The first amended complaint alleges claims under the Song Beverly Act, as well as a claim for fraudulent concealment, which was upheld as against a demurrer. The RFP's, which appear to overlap, all address claims by others regarding engine problems. Furthermore, there appears to be on-going litigation involving these issues. Relevance for discovery purposes is broad and information related to claims in the complaint are discoverable. (CCP §2017.010.) Parties to appear and address issue of relative burden and extent of relevance for purposes of showing good cause when plaintiff's vehicle is over 10 years old and has not experienced any problems. (CCP §2031.010.) Also parties should be prepared to discuss if the analysis differs for particular RFP as it appears that RFPs 99, 101, 112-114, 118-122 relate to the timing chain issue and RFPs 102-110 appear to relate to other alleged problems with the engine. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to rkane1089@aol.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: LIBERTY MUTUAL INSURANCE VS. BRENDAN QUINLAN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT/DEFAULT JUDGMENT/LEAVE TO DEFEND
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, November 18, 2019, Line 7, DEFENDANTS BRENDAN QUINLAN, BRENDAN QUINLAN'S MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT/DEFAULT JUDGMENT/LEAVE TO DEFEND Defendants Brendan Quinlan, an individual, and Brendan Quinlan, an individual dba Quinlan Construction's motion to set aside the defaults entered against them on August 23, 2019, is granted. Defendants' showing supports the conclusion that the defaults were taken as a result of mistake, surprise, inadvertence, or excusable neglect because Mr. Quinlan, a non-lawyer who had never done any business with Liberty Mutual Insurance (which is suing as a subrogee), did not understand the summons or why he was being sued. (Quinlan Decl. 2.) "Because the law strongly favors trial and disposition on the merits, any doubts in applying section 473 must be resolved in favor of the party seeking relief from default." (Elston v. City of Turlock (1985) 38 Cal.3d 227, 233.) Moreover, where, as here, the party in default moves promptly to seek relief, and no prejudice to the opposing party will result from setting aside the default and letting the case go to trial on the merits, "very slight evidence will be required to justify a court in setting aside the default." (Id.) Defendants shall remit $1,000 in fees to plaintiff as reasonable fees for obtaining the default. (See Code Civ. Proc., § 473(c)(1)(C); Rogalski v. Nabers Cadillac (1992) 11 Cal.App.4th 816, 823.) Defendants shall file their answer within 10 days. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the Defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)
